infobox.key: Prince Frederick, Duke of York and Albany
infobox.value: (Frederick Augustus; 16 August 1763 – 5 January 1827), was the second son of [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"), [King of the United Kingdom](/wiki/King_of_the_United_Kingdom "King of the United Kingdom") and [Hanover](/wiki/King_of_Hanover "King of Hanover"), and his consort [Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz](/wiki/Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz"). A soldier by profession, from 1764 to 1803 he was [Prince-Bishop of Osnabrück](/wiki/Prince-Bishop_of_Osnabr%C3%BCck "Prince-Bishop of Osnabrück") in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). From the death of his father in 1820 until his own death in 1827, he was the [heir presumptive](/wiki/Heir_presumptive "Heir presumptive") to his elder brother, [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV"), in both the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") and the [Kingdom of Hanover](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hanover "Kingdom of Hanover").

**Prince Frederick, Duke of York and Albany** (Frederick Augustus; 16 August 1763 – 5 January 1827), was the second son of [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"), [King of the United Kingdom](/wiki/King_of_the_United_Kingdom "King of the United Kingdom") and [Hanover](/wiki/King_of_Hanover "King of Hanover"), and his consort [Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz](/wiki/Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz"). A soldier by profession, from 1764 to 1803 he was [Prince-Bishop of Osnabrück](/wiki/Prince-Bishop_of_Osnabr%C3%BCck "Prince-Bishop of Osnabrück") in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). From the death of his father in 1820 until his own death in 1827, he was the [heir presumptive](/wiki/Heir_presumptive "Heir presumptive") to his elder brother, [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV"), in both the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") and the [Kingdom of Hanover](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hanover "Kingdom of Hanover").

Frederick was thrust into the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") at a very early age and was appointed to high command at the age of thirty, when he was given command of a notoriously [ineffectual campaign](/wiki/Low_Countries_theatre_of_the_War_of_the_First_Coalition "Low Countries theatre of the War of the First Coalition") during the [War of the First Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_First_Coalition "War of the First Coalition"), a continental war following the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"). Later, as [Commander-in-Chief](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief_of_the_Forces "Commander-in-Chief of the Forces") during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), he oversaw the reorganisation of the British Army, establishing vital structural, administrative and recruiting reforms[[1]](#cite_note-Glover,_1963,_p.12-1) for which he is credited with having done "more for the army than any one man has done for it in the whole of its history".[[2]](#cite_note-British_Army_1994_p._145-2)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Frederick belonged to the [House of Hanover](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover").[[3]](#cite_note-heath127-3) He was born on 16 August 1763, at [St. James's Palace](/wiki/St._James%27s_Palace "St. James's Palace"), [London](/wiki/London "London").[[3]](#cite_note-heath127-3) His father was the reigning British monarch, [King George III](/wiki/King_George_III "King George III").[[3]](#cite_note-heath127-3) His mother was [Queen Charlotte](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte "Queen Charlotte") (née Princess of Mecklenburg-Strelitz).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

On 27 February 1764, when Frederick was six months old, he became [Prince-Bishop of Osnabrück](/wiki/Prince-Bishop_of_Osnabr%C3%BCck "Prince-Bishop of Osnabrück") upon the death of [Clemens August of Bavaria](/wiki/Clemens_August_of_Bavaria "Clemens August of Bavaria").[[3]](#cite_note-heath127-3) The [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia") stipulated that the city of Osnabrück would alternate between Catholic and Protestant rulers, with the Protestant bishops to be elected from the [cadets](/wiki/Cadet_(genealogy) "Cadet (genealogy)") of the [House of Brunswick-Lüneburg](/wiki/House_of_Brunswick-L%C3%BCneburg "House of Brunswick-Lüneburg").[[5]](#cite_note-odnb-5) The bishopric of Osnabrück came with a substantial income,[[6]](#cite_note-6) which he retained until the city was incorporated into Hanover in 1803 during the [German mediatisation](/wiki/German_mediatisation "German mediatisation"). He was appointed a Knight of the [Most Honourable Order of the Bath](/wiki/Most_Honourable_Order_of_the_Bath "Most Honourable Order of the Bath") on 30 December 1767[[7]](#cite_note-Cokayne-7) and a Knight of the [Order of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter") on 19 June 1771.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Military career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Military career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Frederick%2C_Duke_of_York_1800-1820.jpg/250px-Frederick%2C_Duke_of_York_1800-1820.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frederick,_Duke_of_York_1800-1820.jpg)

The Duke of York

George III decided that his second son would pursue an army career and had him [gazetted](/wiki/Gazette "Gazette") [colonel](/wiki/Colonel "Colonel") on 4 November 1780.[[9]](#cite_note-9) From 1781 to 1787, Prince Frederick lived in Hanover, where he studied (along with his younger brothers, [Prince Edward](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Kent_and_Strathearn "Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn"), [Prince Ernest](/wiki/Ernest_Augustus,_King_of_Hanover "Ernest Augustus, King of Hanover"), [Prince Augustus](/wiki/Prince_Augustus_Frederick,_Duke_of_Sussex "Prince Augustus Frederick, Duke of Sussex") and [Prince Adolphus](/wiki/Prince_Adolphus "Prince Adolphus")) at the [University of Göttingen](/wiki/University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen "University of Göttingen").[[10]](#cite_note-10) He was appointed colonel of the 2nd Horse Grenadier Guards (later [2nd Life Guards](/wiki/2nd_Life_Guards "2nd Life Guards")) on 26 March 1782[[11]](#cite_note-11) before being promoted to [major-general](/wiki/Major-general "Major-general") on 20 November 1782.[[3]](#cite_note-heath127-3) Promoted to [lieutenant general](/wiki/Lieutenant_general "Lieutenant general") on 27 October 1784,[[3]](#cite_note-heath127-3) he was appointed colonel of the [Coldstream Guards](/wiki/Coldstream_Guards "Coldstream Guards") on 28 October 1784.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

He was created [Duke of York and Albany](/wiki/Duke_of_York_and_Albany "Duke of York and Albany") and [Earl of Ulster](/wiki/Earl_of_Ulster "Earl of Ulster") on 27 November 1784 and became a member of the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Privy Council of the United Kingdom").[[5]](#cite_note-odnb-5) On his return to Great Britain, the Duke took his seat in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), where, on 15 December 1788 during the [Regency crisis](/wiki/George_IV_of_the_United_Kingdom#Regency_crisis_of_1788 "George IV of the United Kingdom"), he opposed [William Pitt](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger")'s Regency Bill in a speech which was supposed to have been influenced by the [Prince of Wales](/wiki/George_IV_of_the_United_Kingdom "George IV of the United Kingdom").[[5]](#cite_note-odnb-5) On 26 May 1789 he took part in a duel with [Colonel Charles Lennox](/wiki/Charles_Lennox,_4th_Duke_of_Richmond "Charles Lennox, 4th Duke of Richmond"), who had insulted him; Lennox missed, and Frederick refused to return fire.[[5]](#cite_note-odnb-5)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Flanders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Flanders")]

Main article: [Flanders campaign](/wiki/Low_Countries_theatre_of_the_War_of_the_First_Coalition "Low Countries theatre of the War of the First Coalition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/The_Grand_Attack_on_Valenciennes.png/250px-The_Grand_Attack_on_Valenciennes.png)](/wiki/File:The_Grand_Attack_on_Valenciennes.png)

*[The Grand Attack on Valenciennes](/wiki/The_Grand_Attack_on_Valenciennes "The Grand Attack on Valenciennes")* by [Philip James de Loutherbourg](/wiki/Philip_James_de_Loutherbourg "Philip James de Loutherbourg"), 1794. The [Siege of Valenciennes](/wiki/Siege_of_Valenciennes_(1793) "Siege of Valenciennes (1793)") was an early Allied success but the campaign soon turned against them.

On 12 April 1793, Frederick was promoted to full general.[[14]](#cite_note-heath128-14) That year, he was sent to [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") in command of the British contingent of [Coburg](/wiki/Prince_Josias_of_Saxe-Coburg-Saalfeld "Prince Josias of Saxe-Coburg-Saalfeld")'s army destined for the invasion of [France](/wiki/France "France").[[14]](#cite_note-heath128-14) Frederick and his command fought in the [Flanders campaign](/wiki/Low_Countries_theatre_of_the_War_of_the_First_Coalition "Low Countries theatre of the War of the First Coalition") under extremely trying conditions. He won several notable engagements, such as the [Siege of Valenciennes](/wiki/Siege_of_Valenciennes_(1793) "Siege of Valenciennes (1793)") in July 1793,[[15]](#cite_note-15) but was defeated at the [Battle of Hondschoote](/wiki/Battle_of_Hondschoote "Battle of Hondschoote") in September 1793.[[14]](#cite_note-heath128-14) In the 1794 campaign he gained a notable success at the [Battle of Beaumont](/wiki/Battle_of_Beaumont_(1794) "Battle of Beaumont (1794)") in April and another at the [Battle of Willems](/wiki/Battle_of_Willems "Battle of Willems") in May but was defeated at the [Battle of Tourcoing](/wiki/Battle_of_Tourcoing "Battle of Tourcoing") later that month.[[14]](#cite_note-heath128-14) The British army was evacuated through [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen "Bremen") in April 1795.[[14]](#cite_note-heath128-14)

### Commander-in-Chief

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Commander-in-Chief")]

See also: [Recruitment in the British Army § Reform](/wiki/Recruitment_in_the_British_Army#Reform "Recruitment in the British Army")

After his return to Britain, his father George III promoted him to the rank of [field marshal](/wiki/Field_marshal_(United_Kingdom) "Field marshal (United Kingdom)") on 18 February 1795.[[14]](#cite_note-heath128-14) On 3 April 1795, George appointed him effective [Commander-in-Chief](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief_of_the_Forces "Commander-in-Chief of the Forces") in succession to [Lord Amherst](/wiki/Lord_Amherst "Lord Amherst")[[16]](#cite_note-p.128,_Glover-16) although the title was not confirmed until three years later.[[17]](#cite_note-17) He was also colonel of the [60th Regiment of Foot](/wiki/60th_Regiment_of_Foot "60th Regiment of Foot") from 19 August 1797.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

On appointment as Commander-in-Chief he immediately declared, reflecting on the [Flanders Campaign](/wiki/Low_Countries_theatre_of_the_War_of_the_First_Coalition "Low Countries theatre of the War of the First Coalition") of 1793–94, "that no officer should ever be subject to the same disadvantages under which he had laboured".[[16]](#cite_note-p.128,_Glover-16)

His second field command was with the army sent for the [Anglo-Russian invasion of Holland](/wiki/Anglo-Russian_invasion_of_Holland "Anglo-Russian invasion of Holland") in August 1799. On 7 September 1799, he was given the honorary title of [Captain-General](/wiki/Captain_general#Commander-in-Chief_of_the_Forces "Captain general").[[19]](#cite_note-19) Sir [Ralph Abercromby](/wiki/Ralph_Abercromby "Ralph Abercromby") and Admiral Sir Charles Mitchell, in charge of the vanguard, had succeeded in capturing some Dutch warships in [Den Helder](/wiki/Den_Helder "Den Helder"). However, following the Duke's arrival with the main body of the army, a number of disasters befell the allied forces, including shortage of supplies.[[20]](#cite_note-heath129-20) On 17 October 1799, the Duke signed the [Convention of Alkmaar](/wiki/Convention_of_Alkmaar "Convention of Alkmaar"), by which the allied expedition withdrew after giving up its prisoners.[[20]](#cite_note-heath129-20) 1799 also saw [Fort Frederick](/wiki/Fort_Frederick,_Eastern_Cape "Fort Frederick, Eastern Cape") in South Africa named after him.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Frederick's military setbacks of 1799 were inevitable given his lack of experience as a field commander, the poor state of the British army at the time, and the conflicting military objectives of the protagonists. After this ineffectual campaign, Frederick was mocked, perhaps unfairly, in the rhyme "[The Grand Old Duke of York](/wiki/The_Grand_Old_Duke_of_York "The Grand Old Duke of York")":

> The grand old Duke of York,  
> He had ten thousand men.  
> He marched them up to the top of the hill  
> And he marched them down again.  
> And when they were up, they were up.  
> And when they were down, they were down.  
> And when they were only halfway up,  
> They were neither up nor down.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Mary_Anne_Clarke2.jpg/250px-Mary_Anne_Clarke2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mary_Anne_Clarke2.jpg)

"The modern Circe or a sequel to the petticoat", caricature of Frederick's lover, [Mary Anne Clarke](/wiki/Mary_Anne_Clarke "Mary Anne Clarke") by [Isaac Cruikshank](/wiki/Isaac_Cruikshank "Isaac Cruikshank"), 15 March 1809. The prince resigned as head of the British army ten days after the caricature's publication.

Frederick's experience in the Dutch campaign made a strong impression on him. That campaign, and the Flanders campaign, had demonstrated the numerous weaknesses of the British army after years of neglect. Frederick as Commander-in-Chief of the British army carried through a massive programme of reform.[[1]](#cite_note-Glover,_1963,_p.12-1) He was the person most responsible for the reforms that created the force which served in the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War"). He was also in charge of the preparations against [Napoleon's planned invasion of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Napoleon%27s_planned_invasion_of_the_United_Kingdom "Napoleon's planned invasion of the United Kingdom") in 1803. In the opinion of Sir [John Fortescue](/wiki/John_Fortescue_(military_historian) "John Fortescue (military historian)"), Frederick did "more for the army than any one man has done for it in the whole of its history".[[2]](#cite_note-British_Army_1994_p._145-2)

In 1801 Frederick actively supported the foundation of the [Royal Military College, Sandhurst](/wiki/Royal_Military_College,_Sandhurst "Royal Military College, Sandhurst"), which promoted the professional, merit-based training of future commissioned officers.[[20]](#cite_note-heath129-20)

In 1801 touched by the plight of children orphaned as a result of the Napoleonic wars, Frederick issued a Royal Warrant and laid the foundation stone in Chelsea to build the Royal Military Asylum (now known as the [Duke of York's Headquarters](/wiki/Duke_of_York%27s_Headquarters "Duke of York's Headquarters")) for orphaned children.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In 1892 the Royal Military Asylum was renamed the [Duke of York's Royal Military School](/wiki/Duke_of_York%27s_Royal_Military_School "Duke of York's Royal Military School"). The school relocated to Dover, Kent in 1909.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

On 14 September 1805 he was given the honorary title of Warden of Windsor Forest.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Frederick resigned as Commander-in-Chief on 25 March 1809, as the result of a scandal caused by the activities of his latest mistress, [Mary Anne Clarke](/wiki/Mary_Anne_Clarke "Mary Anne Clarke").[[20]](#cite_note-heath129-20) Clarke was accused of illicitly selling army commissions under Frederick's aegis.[[20]](#cite_note-heath129-20) A select committee of the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") enquired into the matter. Parliament eventually acquitted Frederick of receiving bribes by 278 votes to 196. He nevertheless resigned because of the high tally against him.[[20]](#cite_note-heath129-20) Two years later, it was revealed that Clarke had received payment for furniture from Frederick's disgraced chief accuser, [Gwyllym Wardle](/wiki/Gwyllym_Wardle "Gwyllym Wardle"),[[26]](#cite_note-26) and the [Prince Regent](/wiki/Prince_regent#Prince_regent_in_the_United_Kingdom "Prince regent") reappointed the exonerated Frederick as Commander-in-Chief on 29 May 1811.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The Duke's relationship with Mary Anne Clarke is used by Mary Anne's descendant, [Daphne du Maurier](/wiki/Daphne_du_Maurier "Daphne du Maurier"), in her historical novel [Mary Anne](/wiki/Mary_Anne_(novel) "Mary Anne (novel)").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Residences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Residences")]

Frederick maintained a country residence at [Oatlands](/wiki/Oatlands_Palace "Oatlands Palace") near [Weybridge](/wiki/Weybridge "Weybridge"), [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey "Surrey") but he was seldom there, preferring to immerse himself in his administrative work at [Horse Guards](/wiki/Horse_Guards_(building) "Horse Guards (building)") (the British army's headquarters) and, after hours, in London's high life, with its gaming tables: Frederick was perpetually in debt because of his excessive gambling on cards and racehorses.[[5]](#cite_note-odnb-5) In 1826 his London residence was [8 South Audley Street, Mayfair](/wiki/8_South_Audley_Street,_Mayfair "8 South Audley Street, Mayfair"), which had previously been occupied by his deceased sister-in-law [Caroline of Brunswick](/wiki/Caroline_of_Brunswick "Caroline of Brunswick").[[29]](#cite_note-HE-List1236395-29)[[30]](#cite_note-KinlochCooke-1900-p19-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Mitton-1903-p14-15-31)

Construction of a palatial London residence for Frederick, [York House (later Lancaster House)](/wiki/Lancaster_House "Lancaster House") commenced in 1825.[[32]](#cite_note-Stourton-32): 155 [Sir Robert Smirke](/wiki/Sir_Robert_Smirke "Sir Robert Smirke") was originally hired to design the house, until under the influence of the Duke's mistress the Duchess of Rutland, he was replaced by [Benjamin Dean Wyatt](/wiki/Benjamin_Dean_Wyatt "Benjamin Dean Wyatt") who mainly designed the exterior.[[32]](#cite_note-Stourton-32): 155 The house was only a shell when Frederick died in 1827. It is constructed from [Bath stone](/wiki/Bath_stone "Bath stone"), in a [neo-classical style](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture"). The [lease](/wiki/Leasehold_estate "Leasehold estate") of the house was purchased from Frederick's executors by the [2nd Marquess of Stafford](/wiki/George_Leveson-Gower,_1st_Duke_of_Sutherland "George Leveson-Gower, 1st Duke of Sutherland") (later 1st [Duke of Sutherland](/wiki/Duke_of_Sutherland "Duke of Sutherland")) and was under his direction that it was completed in 1838.[[33]](#cite_note-listed-33) It was known as Stafford House for almost a century thereafter.[[34]](#cite_note-walford-34)

## Heir presumptive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Heir presumptive")]

Following the unexpected death of his niece, [Princess Charlotte of Wales](/wiki/Charlotte_Augusta "Charlotte Augusta"), in 1817, Frederick became second in line to the throne, with a serious chance of inheriting it.[[35]](#cite_note-heath130-35) In 1820, he became [heir presumptive](/wiki/Heir_presumptive "Heir presumptive") with the death of his father, George III.[[5]](#cite_note-odnb-5)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Death")]

Frederick died of [dropsy](/wiki/Dropsy "Dropsy") and apparent [cardiovascular disease](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease") at the home of the [Duke of Rutland](/wiki/John_Manners,_5th_Duke_of_Rutland "John Manners, 5th Duke of Rutland") in Arlington Street, London, in 1827.[[20]](#cite_note-heath129-20) After [lying in state](/wiki/Lying_in_state "Lying in state") at the [Chapel Royal](/wiki/Chapel_Royal "Chapel Royal") in London,[[34]](#cite_note-walford-34) Frederick's remains were interred in [St George's Chapel, Windsor Castle](/wiki/St_George%27s_Chapel,_Windsor_Castle "St George's Chapel, Windsor Castle"), following his funeral there.[[5]](#cite_note-odnb-5) The chapel was so cold during the funeral, held at night, that the Foreign Secretary, [George Canning](/wiki/George_Canning "George Canning"), contracted [rheumatic fever](/wiki/Rheumatic_fever "Rheumatic fever"), becoming so ill that he thought he might not recover;[[36]](#cite_note-36) Canning died on 8 August the same year.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Family")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Sir_Henry_Singleton_-_The_Marriage_of_the_Duke_and_Duchess_of_York_-_68.46_-_Minneapolis_Institute_of_Arts.jpg/250px-Sir_Henry_Singleton_-_The_Marriage_of_the_Duke_and_Duchess_of_York_-_68.46_-_Minneapolis_Institute_of_Arts.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Henry_Singleton_-_The_Marriage_of_the_Duke_and_Duchess_of_York_-_68.46_-_Minneapolis_Institute_of_Arts.jpg)

*[The Marriage of the Duke of York](/w/index.php?title=The_Marriage_of_the_Duke_of_York&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Marriage of the Duke of York (page does not exist)")* by [Henry Singleton](/wiki/Henry_Singleton_(painter) "Henry Singleton (painter)"), 1791

Frederick married his third cousin Princess [Frederica Charlotte of Prussia](/wiki/Frederica_Charlotte_of_Prussia "Frederica Charlotte of Prussia"), the daughter of King [Frederick William II of Prussia](/wiki/Frederick_William_II_of_Prussia "Frederick William II of Prussia") and [Elisabeth Christine of Brunswick-Lüneburg](/wiki/Elisabeth_Christine_of_Brunswick-L%C3%BCneburg "Elisabeth Christine of Brunswick-Lüneburg"), at Charlottenburg, Berlin, on 29 September 1791 and again on 23 November 1791 at [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace").[[14]](#cite_note-heath128-14) The marriage was not a happy one and the couple soon separated. Frederica retired to [Oatlands Palace](/wiki/Oatlands_Palace "Oatlands Palace"), in Surrey, where she lived until her death in 1820.[[5]](#cite_note-odnb-5)

### Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Honours")]

His honours were as follows:[[40]](#cite_note-gazette-40)

* Royal Knight of the [Order of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter"), *19 June 1771*[[41]](#cite_note-41)
* Knight Grand Cross (military) of the [Order of the Bath](/wiki/Order_of_the_Bath "Order of the Bath"), *2 January 1815*[[42]](#cite_note-42)
* Knight Grand Cross of the [Royal Guelphic Order](/wiki/Royal_Guelphic_Order "Royal Guelphic Order"), *12 August 1815*[[43]](#cite_note-43)
* Knight of the [Order of the Black Eagle](/wiki/Order_of_the_Black_Eagle "Order of the Black Eagle") of Prussia, *11 April 1814*[[44]](#cite_note-44)
* Knight of the [Order of the St-Esprit](/wiki/Order_of_the_Holy_Spirit "Order of the Holy Spirit") of France, *21 April 1814*[[45]](#cite_note-tcp-45)
* Knight of the [Order of St. Andrew](/wiki/Order_of_St._Andrew "Order of St. Andrew") of Russia, *9 June 1814*[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* Knight of the [Order of St. Alexander Nevsky](/wiki/Order_of_St._Alexander_Nevsky "Order of St. Alexander Nevsky") of Russia, *9 June 1814*[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* Knight Grand Cross of the [Order of Charles III](/wiki/Order_of_Charles_III "Order of Charles III") of Spain, *21 August 1814*[[48]](#cite_note-48)
* Knight Grand Cross of the [Order of Maria Theresa](/wiki/Order_of_Maria_Theresa "Order of Maria Theresa") of Austria, *1814*[[45]](#cite_note-tcp-45)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/FrederickDukeofYork594fc.jpg/250px-FrederickDukeofYork594fc.jpg)](/wiki/File:FrederickDukeofYork594fc.jpg)

Statue of Frederick Duke of York in [Waterloo Place](/wiki/Waterloo_Place "Waterloo Place"), [Westminster, London](/wiki/Westminster,_London "Westminster, London")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Duke_of_York_J.Woods_after_a_picture_by_J.Salmon_publ_1837.jpg/250px-Duke_of_York_J.Woods_after_a_picture_by_J.Salmon_publ_1837.jpg)](/wiki/File:Duke_of_York_J.Woods_after_a_picture_by_J.Salmon_publ_1837.jpg)

The [Duke of York Column](/wiki/Duke_of_York_Column "Duke of York Column") seen from [The Mall, London](/wiki/The_Mall,_London "The Mall, London")

[Fredericton](/wiki/Fredericton "Fredericton"), the capital of the [Canadian province](/wiki/Canadian_province "Canadian province") of [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"), was named after Prince Frederick. The city was originally named "Frederick's Town".[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Also in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Duke of York Bay](/wiki/Duke_of_York_Bay "Duke of York Bay"), [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut") was named in his honour, since it was discovered on his birthday, 16 August.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

A statue of Prince Frederick stands in the grounds of Edinburgh Castle, Scotland and was unveiled in 1836. The inscription reads: "Field Marshal His Royal Highness Frederick Duke of York and Albany K.G. Commander and Chief of the British Army MDCCCXXVII."[[51]](#cite_note-51)

In [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia"), [York County](/wiki/York_County,_Western_Australia "York County, Western Australia") and the towns of [York](/wiki/York,_Western_Australia "York, Western Australia") and [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_Western_Australia "Albany, Western Australia") were named after Prince Frederick.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-settlement-53) Albany was originally named "Frederick Town".[[54]](#cite_note-54)

The towering [Duke of York Column](/wiki/Duke_of_York_Column "Duke of York Column") on Waterloo Place, just off [The Mall](/wiki/The_Mall_(London) "The Mall (London)"), London was completed in 1834 as a memorial to Prince Frederick.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

The [72nd Regiment of Foot](/wiki/72nd_Regiment_of_Foot "72nd Regiment of Foot") was given the title *Duke of Albany's Own Highlanders* in 1823 and, in 1881, became 1st Battalion [Seaforth Highlanders (Ross-shire Buffs, The Duke of Albany's)](/wiki/Seaforth_Highlanders_(Ross-shire_Buffs,_The_Duke_of_Albany%27s) "Seaforth Highlanders (Ross-shire Buffs, The Duke of Albany's)").[[56]](#cite_note-56)

The first British fortification in southern Africa, Fort Frederick, [Port Elizabeth](/wiki/Port_Elizabeth "Port Elizabeth"), a city in the Eastern Cape province of South Africa, was built in 1799 to prevent French assistance for rebellious Boers in the short-lived republic of [Graaff-Reinet](/wiki/Graaff-Reinet "Graaff-Reinet").[[57]](#cite_note-57)

The [Duke of York's Royal Military School](/wiki/Duke_of_York%27s_Royal_Military_School "Duke of York's Royal Military School") is named in the duke's honour as he was largely responsible for the founding of the school by Royal Warrant in 1801 (it was originally called the Royal Military Asylum for the Children of Soldiers of the Regular Army). The school was moved to its current site near Dover in 1909. The original building still stands in Chelsea, London.[[58]](#cite_note-58)
